name: Minor in Biomedical Engineering Technology

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: No requirement groups are parsed (requirementGroups 0), so there is no captured plan of study, required core, or elective rules. A genuine applied-BME spine is visible in the pool (BME 111 Physiology -> BME 210 biosignal processing -> BMET 320 Applied Biomedical Data Acquisition -> BMET 415 Biomedical Mechatronics, plus BME 303/304 biomaterials and BME 472 FDA regulation), which is coherent, but it is buried in a much larger unrelated set (nine ACCT accounting courses, FIN 218/315 corporate finance, MGMT 216) that appears drawn from a shared MTSM pool. Without a defined structure the intended progression cannot be judged.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish an explicit minor structure that names the required BMET core (e.g., BME 111, BME 210, BMET 320) and a curated technical elective list, and remove the accounting/finance courses that do not belong in a biomedical-technology minor.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: Prerequisite chains on the biomedical courses are well-formed with explicit grade conditions (e.g., BME 452 requires BME 302, BME 304, MATH 222, MATH 279/333, and BME 321; BMET 320 lists a multi-course foundation), and only two dangling references (ENGR 211, ENGR 312). The deepest chain of 8 is driven by non-BMET courses (CET 435) rather than the biomedical spine, whose chains are moderate. With no requirement groups, however, the graph cannot be tied to an actual minor pathway.
dimensions.suggestion: After defining the required/elective structure, confirm a non-BME minor student can reach BMET 320/415 without hidden deep BME-major chains, and surface the intended entry point (BME 111, which has no prerequisites).

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes appear in the data. For a technology minor that is meant to certify hands-on device competency to non-majors, the absence of any stated outcome is a real gap.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-5 learning outcomes stating what the BMET minor certifies (e.g., operate and interpret biomedical instrumentation, understand medical-device materials and the FDA regulatory pathway, and acquire and process physiological signals).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: There is no credit information: statedTotalCredits, summedTermCredits, and creditReconcileDelta are all null. Per the rubric this is a data-consistency caveat for a thin-data minor rather than an imbalance, but a prospective student cannot see the credit requirement or how required and elective credits are split.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total minor credits (typically 15-18) and list which BME/BMET courses are required versus elective, with credits for each.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The captured entry is incomplete: no plan of study (requirementGroups empty), no credit total, no description, and a pool that opens with roughly nine accounting courses before reaching the biomedical content. A reader cannot discern what the BMET minor consists of.
dimensions.suggestion: Render a clean minor page with a one-paragraph description and the required/elective BMET course list with credits, and fix the parsing so accounting/finance courses do not appear on a biomedical-technology minor.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The genuine BMET courses are notably current and vocational for a technology credential: BME 472 FDA Regulation of Medical Devices covers design documentation and quality management systems, BMET 320 covers MRI/ultrasound/X-ray imaging and physiological sensors (EKG, motion trackers), BMET 415 covers biomedical mechatronics, and BMET 440 provides experiential learning. This aligns well with medical-device industry roles. The score is not higher only because, absent a defined structure, there is no guarantee a minor student reaches these advanced courses.
dimensions.suggestion: Curate a recommended modern track (imaging/instrumentation plus regulatory/quality) so minor students deliberately reach BMET 320, BMET 415, and BME 472 rather than stopping at introductory physiology.

descriptionQuality.justification: The sampled biomedical descriptions are present, specific, and substantive: BMET 320 enumerates imaging modalities and physiological sensors, BME 472 details the FDA regulatory and quality-management process, and BME 452 describes biomaterial properties and failure modes. None of the relevant courses read as thin placeholders.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites on the biomedical courses are stated clearly with grade conditions and explicit multi-course lists (e.g., BMET 320's foundation set, BME 452's five-course prerequisite). Alternatives are sensible (BMET 415 accepts one of several math courses). The main caveat is that some chains are complex for a minor, which the absent structure does not clarify.

missingTopics.topic: Capstone medical-device design and instrumentation lab
missingTopics.rationale: The pool has BMET 320 data acquisition and BMET 415 mechatronics but no explicit integrative device-design capstone; a hands-on design course would convert the theory spine into demonstrable competency.
A biomedical-technology minor should cover the maintenance, calibration, and management of clinical equipment, which no course in the captured pool addresses.
BME 472 covers FDA regulation, but ISO 13485 quality systems and design controls are not surfaced, and IE 461 Product Quality Assurance in the pool is not linked as a device-quality pathway.
BME 210 introduces biosignal processing, but there is no dedicated course on analyzing specific physiological signals, a core technician competency.
BME 304 and BME 452 are theory-oriented; a laboratory course on materials testing and biocompatibility evaluation is missing.

emergingTrends.rationale: BMET 320 already covers EKG and motion sensors; modernizing toward wearable, continuous, and telehealth monitoring reflects where medical-device practice is heading.

emergingTrends.trend: AI/ML in medical imaging and diagnostics
emergingTrends.rationale: BMET 320 covers MRI/ultrasound/X-ray; adding machine-learning image analysis (ENGR 301 data science could feed it) matches the rapid adoption of AI diagnostics.
Connected medical devices demand security competency, which is absent from the pool despite the mechatronics/data-acquisition focus.

emergingTrends.trend: Software as a Medical Device (SaMD) and AI regulation
emergingTrends.rationale: BME 472 covers FDA hardware regulation; extending to software and AI-based device pathways reflects the fastest-growing area of medical-device regulation.

emergingTrends.trend: Additive manufacturing of implants and prosthetics
emergingTrends.rationale: Biomaterials (BME 304/452) plus prototyping (ENGR 320 in the safety pool) could support a 3D-printing/bioprinting elective aligned with current device manufacturing trends.

topSuggestions: Publish the minor's required/elective structure and total credits, which are absent (requirementGroups empty; all credit fields null).
Curate the pool to biomedical courses and remove the roughly nine accounting/finance courses (ACCT 115-435, FIN 218/315) that appear pulled from a shared MTSM course pool.
Add an integrative medical-device design or instrumentation lab so the strong BME/BMET theory spine yields hands-on, demonstrable competency.
Publish 3-5 learning outcomes stating what the BMET minor certifies.
Modernize toward digital health: wearables/IoMT, AI in medical imaging, and Software-as-a-Medical-Device regulation, building on BMET 320 and BME 472.
